curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: direct proportion, inverse proportion, scaling and similar shapes
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Identify and model direct and inverse proportion using y = kx and y = k/x, interpret graphs and constants of proportionality, and apply scaling rules (length, area, volume) to real-world contexts to support accurate and fair representation. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: How can proportion help us model change, scale the world accurately, and represent people and places fairly?

knowing_world.focus: Proportion compares linked quantities. Students distinguish direct vs inverse proportion, use k as the constant of proportionality, connect each to its graph shape, and apply scaling rules for similar shapes (length, area, volume).
key_concepts: Direct proportion graph: straight line through the origin
activities: Quick check: If y = 3x and x = 5, find y.
Reasoning: In direct proportion, if x doubles, what happens to y?
Reasoning: In inverse proportion, if x doubles, what happens to y?
Scaling: A model is 1/25 of real size. If a building is 100 m tall, find the model height.

human_story.focus: Proportion supports recipes, maps, density, speed/time and economics, and helps us judge fairness in representation. Students consider how distorted scaling in maps can introduce bias and misunderstanding.
justice_prompts: Suppose a map shrinks a region disproportionately, exaggerating distances for some areas. What biases or misunderstandings could that introduce?
How does accurate proportional scaling support fairness in representation?
activities: Discussion: collect real examples where proportion errors cause real consequences (maps, dosage, pricing, building plans).

expressive_self.focus: Students express direct and inverse proportion through sketching, monologue, and spoken explanation, linking responsibility and meaning to scaling and rate relationships.
activities: Sketch two versions of a room: real 5 m × 4 m, and a 1/20 scale model; label corresponding lengths.
Write a short monologue: “If you triple the dose in direct proportion, what responsibility comes with that?”
Record a 60-second voice note explaining inverse proportion using speed vs time (if speed doubles, travel time halves).

designed_world.focus: Students use an interactive visualiser to match inputs and outputs in direct and inverse proportion, explore patterns using specific formulas, and invent their own proportional model from a real situation.
tools: Canva embed: interactive direct & inverse proportion visualiser and matching tool
activities: Explore direct proportion with y = 2x for x = 5, 10, 20; record y-values and describe the pattern.
Switch to inverse proportion with y = 100/x; try x = 2, 5, 10; describe what changes and what stays constant.
Use the matching tool to connect x and y values with their correct formulas; check your reasoning.
Invent a real situation (recipe, journey, model) and describe it using direct or inverse proportion.
design_challenges: Create a real-world scaling or rate scenario and model it using either y = kx or y = k/x; justify your choice and interpret k.

regulation_supports: Stretch hands in proportion: reach one arm wide, then the other, repeating in rhythm.
Walk/pacing: 1 minute focusing on pace; connect speed as distance/time.
wellbeing_prompts: When proportion problems feel confusing, what small reset helps you return to the relationship calmly?

pathways.name: Final Task - Direct vs Inverse Proportion Mini-Guide
pathways.focus: Create a mini-guide or poster explaining direct vs inverse proportion with examples and a solved scaling problem.
products: A mini-guide/poster explaining direct proportion and inverse proportion
3 clear examples of direct proportion with correct equations and/or graphs
3 clear examples of inverse proportion with correct equations and/or graphs
One real-world scaling problem solved step-by-step (map/model/recipe/time-speed)
A short reflection on which type was trickier and how you resolved it
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding — Explains both types clearly using equations and/or graphs
Fluency & Accuracy — Correct calculations and correct identification of proportional relationships
Application — Uses proportion in a real-world scaling/rate context with clear steps
Independence & Reflection — Reflects on difficulty and strategy used to improve

required: Using multiplication and division with integers and decimals

builds_toward: Ratio and proportion problem solving (including best buys and scaling)
Speed, density and pressure-style unit problems
Similar shapes and scale factor reasoning
Graphs and functions (interpreting k and graph shapes)
